solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1488I - Demonic Invasion
First of all, since mages are only one turn ahead of the horde, they must travel from node $$$1$$$ as far as possible, so let's construct the shortest path graph with BFS. The shortest path graph is constructed as follows: find the distances from $$$1$$$ to all other nodes, and for each edge from $$$x$$$ to $$$y$$$, add a directed arc from $$$x$$$ to $$$y$$$ if $$$d_x = d_y - 1$$$ (where $$$d_x$$$ is the distance from $$$1$$$ to $$$x$$$). Then, the mages should travel along the arcs of this directed acyclic graph.
Since each stone decays in $$$2$$$ days, a mage is able to perform a ritual only if they have taken two stones along two consecutive arcs in a path. Let's split all arcs into two parts: the ones leading from vertices with odd distances, and the ones leading from vertices with even distances. We can pair up two arcs from different parts if they share a vertex, so this problem can be reformulated as an instance of a bipartite maximum matching.
Unfortunately, even constructing this bipartite graph is quite costly (it may have up to $$$O(m^2)$$$ edges), let alone running Kuhn's algorithm on it. Let's try to design a flow solution. Let's add a source and a sink to our network (I call them $$$s$$$ and $$$t$$$ respectively). Split each arc by adding a new vertex $$$v$$$ and three arcs in our flow network:
if the arc was going from the vertex $$$x$$$ with even distance to the vertex $$$y$$$ with odd distance, we add three arcs $$$(s, v)$$$, $$$(v, x)$$$ and $$$(v, y)$$$ (to model the fact that this arc can be paired with some other arc connected to $$$x$$$ or $$$y$$$);
if the arc was going from the vertex $$$x$$$ with odd distance to the vertex $$$y$$$ with even distance, we add three arcs $$$(x, v)$$$, $$$(y, v)$$$ and $$$(v, t)$$$ (to model the same thing).
If the flow goes from $$$s$$$ to one of the vertices, it means that we have paired the respective arc (the same for the case when the flow goes from one of the vertices to $$$t$$$). So, the maximum flow $$$f$$$ in this network is the maximum number of pairs of arcs we can merge together. Thus, the answer to the problem is $$$\min(k, f)$$$.
The maximum flow can be computed in $$$O(m^{1.5})$$$ by using the Dinic algorithm since we have a unit network.
